planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in fall or plant container-grown plants in fall or winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full shade to part shade; evergreen currant is native to the understory of Coast Live Oak woodland and is adapted to summer-dry conditions under tree canopy. It requires excellent drainage and may struggle in wet clay soils. Established plants need no supplemental water in summer.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; evergreen currant is one of the finest California native groundcovers for dry shade under oaks and other native trees — a low, spreading shrub from Santa Catalina and Anacapa islands and the adjacent mainland coast that thrives in the same summer-dry, shaded conditions as Coast Live Oak woodland where few other plants succeed. The rounded, dark-green, leathery leaves have an appealing, aromatic, resinous fragrance when crushed. In late winter and early spring it produces small clusters of tiny, deep-wine-red to rose-pink flowers that are disproportionately attractive to hummingbirds for their size. The plant requires essentially no summer water once established and actually resents summer irrigation, making it outstanding for planting beneath oaks where summer watering risks fatal Phytophthora infections of the oaks. An essential plant for California native gardens and water-wise landscapes requiring shade coverage without summer irrigation.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in fall or plant container-grown plants in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, lean soil in full sun to part shade; fuchsia-flowering gooseberry requires summer dormancy and must not be watered after late spring. It is spiny and forms impenetrable thickets — an advantage as a barrier planting but a consideration near paths.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; fuchsia-flowering gooseberry is a California native coastal shrub producing one of the most spectacular winter-flowering displays of any western native plant — long, pendant, brilliant-red flowers (dangling on slender stalks in clusters of 1–4) from January through March that are, in form and color, almost identical to a fuchsia flower: deeply red, tubular, with projecting red stamens that extend beyond the tube. These winter-blooming flowers appear on nearly leafless or semi-leafless stems and are an overwhelmingly important resource for Anna's and Allen's hummingbirds during the period when almost no other nectar sources are available. The plant goes fully dormant in summer — browning its leaves and appearing dead, which is entirely normal. The thorny stems create an excellent barrier planting and wildlife shelter. A startlingly beautiful winter-blooming native shrub for California and Pacific Coast gardens in zones 8b–10.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rock rose requires excellent drainage and resents summer irrigation once established. It is relatively short-lived (7–15 years) but often self-sows. The sticky aromatic leaves produce labdanum resin that has been collected for perfumery since antiquity.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; crimson-spot rock rose is the largest-flowered of the Mediterranean cistus shrubs — producing blooms 3–4 inches across in pure white, each petal bearing a prominent crimson-maroon basal blotch that creates a ring of five vivid spots within the white flower, giving each bloom the quality of a hand-painted botanical illustration. The flowers last only one day each (the petals falling by late afternoon), but are produced in such lavish succession from May through July that the plant appears constantly in flower for weeks. The sticky, dark-green, lance-shaped leaves produce labdanum resin with a distinctive, warm, ambery-woody fragrance that has been harvested for use in perfumery since ancient times — the gum being collected by raking the hillside goats' beards after they browse among the plants. Outstanding as a drought-tolerant flowering shrub for lean, rocky, or sandy soils in hot, dry gardens in zones 8b–10.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown plants in sharply drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver bush requires excellent drainage — poorly drained or wet soil causes root rot and death. It is one of the most drought-tolerant garden shrubs and requires little to no water once established in appropriate conditions.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver bush is a compact Mediterranean shrub of the bindweed family prized for its outstanding foliage — small, narrow, lance-shaped leaves so densely covered with fine, silky, silver hairs that the entire plant appears to be fashioned from spun silver, creating a brilliant, luminous effect in the hot, sunny garden. From spring through summer it produces successive flushes of typical bindweed-shaped flowers, each a pure white funnel with a faint pink exterior, emerging from distinctly pinkish buds and opening into clean white — the contrast of the metallic silver foliage and snow-white flowers being extraordinary. A Mediterranean cliff and limestone plant from rocky coastal scrub, it requires the same lean, dry, well-drained, sunny conditions as lavender and rosemary and performs best with no summer irrigation in appropriate climates. Outstanding in combination with other silver-leafed and drought-tolerant Mediterranean plants in water-wise gardens.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; Spanish rockrose requires excellent drainage and tolerates no waterlogging. It is closely related to Cistus and requires similar cultivation: lean soil, full sun, no summer water once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Spanish rockrose is a close relative of the cistus family shrubs, native to dry, sandy, and rocky scrublands of the Iberian Peninsula, producing spreading mounds of gray-green to silver-gray aromatic foliage and in late spring through early summer, vivid bright-yellow flowers 1–2 inches across, each with a distinctive dark reddish-brown to purple-black basal blotch on each petal — a spot pattern producing a strong eye at the center of each flower in dramatic contrast to the yellow. The dark basal markings against clear yellow create a flower of considerable visual impact. Like the cistuses, each flower lasts only a day, but is replaced in rapid succession over several weeks. Often crossed with Cistus species to produce the hybrid genus x Halimiocistus, which shows the qualities of both parents. Outstanding for lean, dry, hot sunny gardens in zones 8b–10, particularly in combination with other Mediterranean scrub plants.
